items.q: Can a single standard electrode potential be measured in isolation?
items.correct: No — electrode potentials are RELATIVE values measured against a reference (the standard hydrogen electrode, defined as 0 V).
items.misconception: Each electrode has an absolute potential that can be measured directly with a voltmeter.
items.random_wrong: Standard electrode potentials are measured by submerging the electrode in pure water.
items.why: Students think a voltmeter can give a single electrode's value; they don't grasp that potential difference requires two electrodes by definition.

items.q: If E°cell for a redox reaction is positive, what does that tell you?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — but it may still be very slow if kinetic barriers are high.
items.misconception: A positive E°cell guarantees the reaction will happen rapidly at room temperature.
items.random_wrong: A positive E°cell means the reaction is exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students equate 'feasible' with 'fast', missing the thermodynamics-vs-kinetics distinction (same error as with negative ΔG).

items.q: Why is the hydration enthalpy of a small, highly charged ion very exothermic?
items.correct: Small/highly charged ions form strong ion-dipole attractions with water molecules, releasing a large amount of energy.
items.misconception: Hydration enthalpy only depends on the size of the ion, not its charge.
items.random_wrong: Hydration enthalpy is the same for all ions because water molecules are identical.
items.why: Students focus on one factor (size OR charge) when actually both matter — charge density determines the strength of ion-dipole interactions.

items.q: EMF of a cell is calculated by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: E°cathode − E°anode. “E°anode − E°cathode” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 96,500 C/mol. “6.02 × 10²³ C/mol” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Electrolysis of AgNO₃ for 965 s at 1.00 A. Moles of Ag deposited (Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag):
items.why: Exact answer: 0.01. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
